stickyNotes.text:
When two features are perfectly correlated (e.g., predicting house prices using both Size in Sq Ft and a second column that is just Size in Sq Ft * 0.092 for square meters), they are linearly dependent.

Geometrically, instead of adding a new dimension of information, the second feature points in the exact same direction as the first. The model is trying to solve an equation where it allocates "importance" (weights) to each feature, but it has no math-based way to decide how to split the credit between two identical signals    

The "Near-Dependent" Nightmare (Multicollinearity): In the real world, features are rarely perfectly identical; they are just highly correlated (e.g., 99%). In this case, the determinant is not zero, but it is an incredibly tiny decimal (like $0.00001$). a small change like 1 unit shift to make 93 a 94 caould create a massive change in prediction

How to Fix It in Practice: Dimensionality Reduction, Regularization (L2 / Ridge Regression).

When a matrix cannot be inverted, it means information has been permanently smashed together and lost. In AI, this breaks the model’s ability to learn unique weights or find stable paths to a solution.

If a scalar were natural number like 1, 2, 3... and if we consider vector as a point, they would create a perfect grid structure. Imagine 2D, 3D, 2D span in 3D space would look like a flat sheet in space # 2d span in 3d space span: all possible vectors from scalar vector **Here, vectors are seen as points and all points are connected through lines, creating a 3D grid structure, the basis of 3d programming** 2D span is moving through 3D space with 3rd vector
basis are like vector on x, y, z line of a graph (space).

# "How much of vector $\vec{a}$ is pointing in the exact same direction as vector $\vec{b}$?" n PCA, you have high-dimensional data (say, 100 features) and want to project it down to 2 or 3 principal axes to visualize it.

Remember when we talked about matrices losing their inverses because columns aren't independent? Projections are how we fix that! If you have two vectors that aren't perpendicular, you can take the second vector, subtract its projection onto the first, and boom: you are left with a purely perpendicular component. This is how algorithms force matrices to have perfectly independent, orthogonal columns (making them easily invertible).

Normalizing means changing a vector's length to exactly 1 (turning it into a unit vector) while keeping its **direction exactly the same.** Why do this in AI? When dealing with text embeddings or features, you often care about the direction of the data (what it means) rather than the magnitude (how long the document is). Normalizing puts all data on equal footing
Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle $\theta$ between two vectors. It tells you how closely two vectors point in the same direction, completely ignoring their scale.
Matrix-Vector Multiplication (transforming a vector). Matrix-Matrix Multiplication (composing transformations).

flashcardNotes.frontText: A basis is a minimal set of linearly independent vectors that span the entire space (what do you understand?).
flashcardNotes.backText:
think of a basis as the "efficiency sweet spot" for defining a vector space:

Linearly Independent (No Redundancy): You don't have any extra, useless vectors. No vector in the set can be built from a combination of the others. If you remove even one, you lose the ability to reach certain parts of the space.

Spans the Space (Complete Coverage): The vectors can reach every single point in the entire vector space through linear combinations (scaling and adding them together). If you added another vector to the set, it would automatically be redundant.

The standard basis is $i = [1, 0]$ and $j = [0, 1]$.They are linearly independent (one points purely right, one points purely up; you can't make $j$ out of $i$).

They span the space (you can reach any coordinate $(x, y)$ by doing $x(i) + y(j)$).If you only had $i$, you couldn't span the space (you're stuck on the x-axis). 

If you had $i$, $j$, and a diagonal vector like $[1, 1]$, they would still span the space, but they are no longer a basis because they aren't minimally independent (the third vector is just $i + j$).

flashcardNotes.frontText: The matrix cannot be inverted (why, importance, where)A non-invertible matrix means information has been permanently lost during the transformation.
flashcardNotes.backText:
A non-invertible matrix means information has been **permanently lost** during the transformation. transformation cant be undone

---

## Why Does a Matrix Become Non-Invertible?
matrix fails to have an inverse when its columns (or rows) are not linearly independent

### Mathematically, it happens when:
The Determinant is Zero ($\det(A) = 0$): 
The determinant measures how much a matrix changes the volume of a space. If the determinant is $0$, the matrix squashes a 2D area into a 1D line, or a 3D volume into a 2D flat plane. You cannot inflate a flat line back into a 2D space without guessing.

### Redundant Columns: 
One column is just a combination of the others. For example:$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$The second column is just $2 \times$ the first column. They point in the exact same direction, failing to span the 2D space.
